title: The Word of the Cross
speaker: Pastor Tito Lyro
source_files: 02-23-25 -AM- The Word of the Cross - 1 Corintians 1_18-23 - Pastor Tito Lyro.txt

outline.title: Introduction: The Cross as the Symbol of Christianity
subpoints: Every major religion has a symbol: lotus (Buddhism), crescent (Islam), Star of David (Judaism), hammer and sickle (Communism), swastika (Nazism) — and Christianity: the cross
No marketing agency would have chosen the cross — it was the symbol of weakness, death, criminality, and persecution
The cross became Christianity's symbol not for its appearance but for what it represents: the work of Christ
Remove the cross and you don't have biblical Christianity; unless we are cross-centered, we don't have it

outline.title: 1. The Word of the Cross Separates: Perishing vs. Being Saved (vv. 18-19)
subpoints: 'The message of the cross is foolishness to those who are perishing, but to us who are being saved it is the power of God' (v.18)
The 'word of the cross' = the gospel of Jesus Christ; Paul preached only 'Christ and him crucified' — all other topics were through that lens (v.23, 2:2)
God designed the word to have this separating effect — Paul quotes Isaiah 29:14: God brings the wisdom of the wise to nothing by working in ways they never anticipated

The same gospel smells like death to those perishing and like life to those being saved (2 Cor. 2:15-16); the same preaching on Pentecost moved some to faith and others to mock 'they are full of new wine' (Acts 2:11-13)

outline.title: 2. The Word of the Cross Turns Wisdom into Foolishness and Foolishness into Wisdom (vv. 20-25)
subpoints: The wise man (Greek philosopher), the scribe (Jewish expert), the disputer of this age — Paul is not afraid of any of them; his message will stand against anything man has to offer
The wisdom of this world says: power and might are where glory is found; humility and meekness are weakness; self-esteem is essential

God did not allow the world's wise to find him through their own wisdom — God knew that human arrogance would be intolerable if men could say 'we found God' (Matthew 11:25 — 'You have hidden these things from the wise and revealed them to babes')

Jews demanded signs; Greeks sought philosophical wisdom — Paul gave them Christ crucified: a scandal to Jews (a failed Messiah) and stupidity to Greeks (a savior who couldn't argue his way off the cross)

outline.title: 3. The Word of the Cross Turns Nobodies into Sons and Daughters of God (vv. 26-31)
subpoints: 'Not many wise according to the flesh, not many mighty, not many noble are called' — God chooses the foolish, weak, base, and despised things of the world
Why? So that no one can boast before God — God does not need us; he uses us, and that's the glory of it
The church is a hospital for ailing sinners, not a hotel for perfect citizens (Augustine) — we are all misfits; stop faking
In Christ Jesus, God himself became our wisdom, righteousness, sanctification, and redemption — so that he who glories may glory in the Lord (Jeremiah 9:23-24; Galatians 6:14)

summary: In place of the scheduled Hosea 2 sermon, Pastor Tito Lyro preaches on 1 Corinthians 1:18-31 — Paul's exposition of 'the word of the cross.' The morning covers the explanation (the afternoon covers the application). The cross, originally a symbol of weakness and death, became Christianity's central symbol not for its appearance but for what it represents. Paul's text teaches three things: the word of the cross separates the perishing (who find it foolish) from the being saved (who find it the power of God); it inverts the world's categories of wisdom and foolishness; and it turns the world's nobodies into sons and daughters of God — all so that boasting may be in the Lord alone, not in human wisdom, might, or pedigree.
transcript:
## Introduction: The Cross and Its Symbol

We're going to look at 1 Corinthians chapter 1 this morning instead of Hosea chapter 2 — it just didn't want to come out. We'll return to Hosea 2 next Lord's Day. This morning will be the explanation of these verses; this afternoon will be the application.

Every major ideology has its symbol. Buddhism: the lotus flower. Islam: the crescent. Judaism: the Star of David. Confucianism: the yin and yang. Communism: the hammer and sickle. The Nazi movement: the swastika. And Christianity is known by the cross.

The cross, especially the empty cross, has been the symbol of Christianity from very early in history. It was not the earliest symbol — the fish, the burning bush, and other motifs were used — but the cross is early enough and fairly universal that we can call it Christianity's symbol.

It was not a well-chosen symbol, from a marketing standpoint. If you had hired a marketing agency in the first century and they came back with a cross as your logo, you should fire them. The cross was not a symbol of power. It was a symbol of weakness, death, criminal punishment, and persecution. Nobody looked at the cross and felt inspired. When Jesus used the imagery of the cross — *take up your cross daily and follow me* (Luke 9:23) — he was not talking about suffering vaguely. He was talking about dying: *Die to yourself daily. That is the only commitment I require.*

The cross became Christianity's symbol not because of how it looks but because of what it represents: the work of the Lord Jesus Christ. The work of Christ was at the center of Christ's own preaching and at the center of the apostles' preaching. Remove what the cross signifies and you don't have biblical Christianity anymore. We can talk about being a better person, a better husband, successful living — but none of that is the core. The cross of Jesus is at the center of who we are as a people.

## 1. The Word of the Cross Separates (vv. 18-19)

> *For the message of the cross is foolishness to those who are perishing, but to us who are being saved, it is the power of God. For it is written: 'I will destroy the wisdom of the wise and bring to nothing the understanding of the prudent.'*

First, what is the 'word of the cross'? Paul defines it in verse 17: the gospel of Jesus Christ. He develops it in verse 23: *we preach Christ crucified.* And in 2:2: *I determined not to know anything among you except Jesus Christ and him crucified.* Paul says that is all he told the Corinthians — and yet 1 Corinthians covers court disputes, marriage, spiritual gifts, love, the Lord's Supper. So it is about all those things, but through the lens of Christ crucified.

Everyone who has ever lived falls into two groups: those who are perishing, who find the message of a crucified Savior foolish, and those who are being saved, to whom it is the very power of God. Leon Morris writes: *The message of the cross is not simply good advice telling us what we should do. Nor is it information about God's power. It is God's power.* You want to see the power of God? Look at floods and volcanoes — that's impressive. But the most powerful thing God ever did is work through his gospel to save sinners like you and me. It takes more for God to save you than to create the universe, because it required giving his only Son on your behalf.

We see this separation in Acts 2. At Pentecost, people from every nation heard the apostles preaching. Some were amazed: *We hear them speaking in our own tongues the wonderful works of God.* Others, mocking: *They are full of new wine.* The same event — faith in some, mockery in others. The word of God has an effect on everyone who hears it.

This separating effect was designed by God. Paul quotes Isaiah 29:14: *I will destroy the wisdom of the wise and bring to nothing the understanding of the prudent.* God did not allow the world's wise to find him through their own wisdom — and there is a reason for that.

## 2. The Word of the Cross Inverts Wisdom and Foolishness (vv. 20-25)

> *Where is the wise? Where is the scribe? Where is the disputer of this age? Has not God made foolish the wisdom of this world? For since, in the wisdom of God, the world through wisdom did not know God, it pleased God through the foolishness of the message preached to save those who believe.*

Paul is not afraid of the best thinkers the world has to offer. He invites them: bring your ideas. His worldview will stand the test of everything man has to offer. None of it will disprove the gospel of Jesus Christ. None of it will reduce its power.

The wisdom of this world says: humility, meekness, and selflessness are weakness; power and might are where glory is found; self-esteem is essential. And this wisdom drives the world away from God. The first generation raised in the 'everyone is a winner' system is now adult — and it's not working, because no one else agrees that you're number one.

God did not allow the world's wise to find him through their own wisdom. Why? Paul Barnett: *In the depths of God's wisdom, he did not allow the wise of Greece or the scribes of Israel to know him through their wisdom. God knew that human arrogance would be intolerable if people from this corrupt age could reach up with their intellect and say, 'There is God — we found him.'* This is God who finds you, not you who find God. Matthew 11:25: Jesus praying to his Father: *You have hidden these things from the wise and prudent and revealed them to babes.*

Jews demanded signs — even though he was feeding five thousand, raising the dead, casting out demons, healing the sick, calming storms. They said: give us a sign. The only sign given is the sign of Jonah — three days in the belly of the fish, three days in the belly of the earth, resurrection. That is the only sign needed.

Greeks sought wisdom in the tradition of the philosopher-king — education as the savior of mankind. Much of the Western world still believes this, even some in the church — to the point of sacrificing children to a godless educational system. There is only one Savior of mankind.

Paul gave them the only thing he could: Christ crucified. A scandal to Jews, stupidity to Greeks — but to those who are called, both Jews and Greeks, *Christ the power of God and the wisdom of God.* And notice: the message of the cross transcends ethnic and cultural barriers. Jews and Greeks — meaning anyone regardless of ethnicity or social status. That is the power of the gospel.

## 3. The Word of the Cross Turns Nobodies into Sons and Daughters (vv. 26-31)

> *For you see your calling, brethren, that not many wise according to the flesh, not many mighty, not many noble, are called. But God has chosen the foolish things of the world to put to shame the wise, and God has chosen the weak things of the world to put to shame the things which are mighty.*

When Paul says 'not many,' he means what he says — not many wise, not many powerful, not many noble. The Church of Jesus Christ is mostly made up of nobodies who have been redeemed by God through the message of the cross. That goes against how we tend to think of ourselves.

Why does God do it this way? So that *no flesh should glory in his presence* (v.29). God does not need us. If I were gone tomorrow, the Church of Jesus Christ would continue without a blip. God doesn't need us — but he uses us, and that is the beauty and glory of it. He uses misfits to build one another in faith.

Origen, writing in the early centuries, observed something striking about Christianity versus other religions. Every other mystery religion said: *Come to us if you have clean hands, a wise tongue, and a life lived well.* Christianity said: *Whoever is a sinner, whoever is unwise, whoever is a child, whoever is wretched — the kingdom of God will receive them.* The church is a hospital for ailing sinners, not a hotel for perfect citizens. Stop faking it. We are all messed up — and that is exactly what God chose.

The transformation that happens through the cross is described in verse 30: *Of him you are in Christ Jesus, who became for us wisdom from God — and righteousness and sanctification and redemption.* Everything we need for our standing before God, everything we need for our growth, everything we need for our final delivery — it is all in Christ.

The purpose: *That he who glories, let him glory in the Lord* (v.31; Jeremiah 9:23-24). Let not the wise man glory in wisdom, the mighty man in might, the rich man in riches — but let him who glories glory in this: that he understands and knows God. The Christian knows what he has been saved from and saved into — and boasts about his God. Paul in Galatians 6:14: *God forbid that I should boast except in the cross of our Lord Jesus Christ.*

When you go about your life, what are you boasting about? Is it Christ? Does the cross permeate every communication, every conversation, every thought? That is the place Christ should have in us.

Father, thank you for speaking through the cross. Help us to love and believe in the word of the cross. In Jesus' name, amen.
